content:
# Exodus Chapter 8 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Filed:** 2026-03-04

---

## 1. v.1 -- *Shalach et-ammi*: "Send out my people" vs. "Let my people go"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Send out my people, and let them serve me" | Literal: imperative *shalach* rendered directly |
| LOGOS | "Send out My people, that they may serve Me" | Formal: imperative + formal purpose clause |
| PNEUMA | "Let my people go, so they may serve me" | Dynamic: culturally resonant English phrasing |

**Hebrew:** שַׁלַּח אֶת־עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי (*shalach et-ammi veya'avduni*) -- piel imperative of *shalach* ("to send, to release") + vav-consecutive imperfect of *avad* ("to serve").

**LXX:** ἐξαπόστειλον τὸν λαόν μου ἵνα μοι λατρεύσωσιν (*exaposteilon ton laon mou hina moi latreusōsin*) -- "send out my people that they may serve me." The Greek uses the strong compound *exaposteilon* ("send out completely"), confirming the active imperative force.

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA's "Let my people go" is the most culturally recognizable phrasing in English, owing to the KJV tradition and the African American spiritual tradition. However, it substitutes a permissive construction ("let... go") for an imperative command ("send out!"). The Hebrew *shalach* is a direct order -- Moses commands Pharaoh to act, not to permit. "Let go" implies Pharaoh holds the power and is being asked to release it; "send out" implies YHWH holds the power and Pharaoh is being told to execute the order. The purpose clause also differs: EMET's "and let them serve me" uses a permissive ("let"); LOGOS's "that they may serve me" uses a formal subjunctive; PNEUMA's "so they may serve me" is the most casual.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Send out my people, that they may serve me" -- following LOGOS's structure with SOPHIA's lowercase convention. The imperative force of *shalach* is preserved, and the formal purpose clause renders *veya'avduni* with appropriate weight.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The meaning is ultimately the same (release the people for worship), but the register and theology of agency differ. "Send out" frames Pharaoh as a subordinate executing a divine command; "let go" frames him as a sovereign granting permission. The Hebrew supports the former.

---

## 2. v.9 -- *Hitpa'er alai*: "The honor is yours" vs. "Glorify yourself over me" vs. "Glory over me!"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Glorify yourself over me" | Literal: hitpael of *pa'ar* + *alai* rendered directly |
| LOGOS | "Glory over me!" | Formal: compressed but still opaque |
| PNEUMA | "The honor is yours -- set the time!" | Dynamic: interpretive, pragmatic sense |

**Hebrew:** הִתְפָּאֵר עָלַי (*hitpa'er alai*) -- hitpael of *pa'ar* ("to glorify, beautify oneself") + *al* + first person suffix ("over me, upon me").

**LXX:** τάξαι πρός με (*taxai pros me*) -- "appoint for me [a time]." The Greek interprets the phrase as an invitation to set a deadline, losing the *pa'ar* root entirely.

**Nature of disagreement:** This is one of the most debated phrases in the frog plague narrative. The literal renderings (EMET, LOGOS) preserve the Hebrew roots but produce English that is unintelligible without commentary. A reader encountering "Glorify yourself over me" or "Glory over me!" cannot determine what Moses is asking Pharaoh to do. PNEUMA's "The honor is yours" captures the pragmatic sense -- Moses is deferring the choice to Pharaoh as a demonstration of power -- while preserving the *pa'ar* root in the English word "honor" (which is semantically adjacent to "glory"). The added "set the time!" is PNEUMA's interpolation, not in the Hebrew, but the following clause (*le-matai a'tir lekha*, "for when shall I entreat for you?") makes the time-setting sense explicit.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The honor is yours" -- following PNEUMA. This is a rare case where SOPHIA favors the dynamic rendering over the literal, because the literal English fails to function as communication. The colon that follows ("for when shall I entreat...") restores the Hebrew structure without needing PNEUMA's added "set the time!" The root *pa'ar* is preserved through "honor."

**Severity:** HIGH. The three renderings offer genuinely different reading experiences. EMET/LOGOS produce mystery; PNEUMA produces clarity. The Hebrew itself is unusual and may have been opaque even to ancient readers, as the LXX's complete reinterpretation (*taxai*) suggests.

---

## 3. v.15 -- *Kaved* hiphil: Pharaoh as active agent of his own hardening

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he made heavy his heart" | Literal: Hebrew word order preserved |
| LOGOS | "he made heavy his heart" | Formal: same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "he made his heart heavy" | Dynamic: English-natural word order |

**Hebrew:** וְהַכְבֵּד אֶת־לִבּוֹ (*vehakhbed et-libbo*) -- hiphil infinitive absolute of *kaved* functioning as a finite verb, with Pharaoh as agent.

**LXX:** ἐβάρυνεν τὴν καρδίαν αὐτοῦ (*ebarunen tēn kardian autou*) -- "he made heavy his heart." The Greek confirms Pharaoh's agency and the causative force.

**Nature of disagreement:** The disagreement is purely about word order, not meaning. All three correctly identify Pharaoh as the agent. EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew verb-object order ("made heavy [verb-adj] his heart [object]"); PNEUMA uses natural English order ("made his heart [object] heavy [complement]"). The question is whether the front-loading of "made heavy" before "his heart" carries theological weight -- emphasizing the *act* of making heavy before naming what is made heavy.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he made heavy his heart" -- following EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew's emphatic construction warrants preserving the unusual English word order. This is the first *kaved* hiphil with Pharaoh as agent in the plague cycle; the rendering should signal its weight.

**Severity:** LOW. All three convey the same meaning and agency. The disagreement is stylistic.

---

## 4. vv.16-17 -- *Kinnim*: "gnats" vs. "gnats/lice" (identification)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "kinnim [gnats/lice]" | Literal: transliterated with dual gloss, preserving ambiguity |
| LOGOS | "gnats" | Formal: follows LXX identification |
| PNEUMA | "gnats" | Dynamic: follows LXX identification |

**Hebrew:** כִּנִּם / כִּנָּם (*kinnim / kinnam*) -- identity disputed: gnats, lice, mosquitoes, sand-flies.

**LXX:** σκνῖφες (*skniphes*) -- "gnats/mosquitoes."

**Talmud:** Sanhedrin 67b identifies *kinnim* as lice.

**Nature of disagreement:** The identification of *kinnim* is a genuine lexical ambiguity. The LXX and the plague's origin from dust (*afar ha-arets*) favor gnats/midges. The Talmudic tradition favors lice. EMET preserves the ambiguity with a dual gloss; LOGOS and PNEUMA commit to "gnats." The question is whether a translation text should resolve ambiguity or preserve it.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "gnats" -- following LOGOS and PNEUMA. The LXX evidence and the dust-origin favor gnats. The ambiguity is preserved in the footnote. EMET's transliteration-plus-gloss approach is more appropriate for a study edition than a translation text.

**Severity:** LOW. The specific insect identity does not affect the theological meaning. What matters narratively is that (a) the plague originates from the dust of the earth, (b) the magicians cannot replicate it, and (c) they confess "the finger of God." These points are unaffected by whether the creatures are gnats or lice.

---

## 5. v.19 -- "The finger of God": word order and punctuation

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A finger of Elohim [God] [is] this" | Literal: Hebrew predicate-subject order |
| LOGOS | "This is the finger of God" | Formal: English subject-predicate order |
| PNEUMA | "This is the finger of God!" | Dynamic: English order + exclamation mark |

**Hebrew:** אֶצְבַּע אֱלֹהִים הִוא (*etsba Elohim hi*) -- predicate (*etsba Elohim*, "a finger of God") + demonstrative pronoun (*hi*, "this/it [is]"). Hebrew places the predicate first for emphasis.

**Nature of disagreement:** EMET preserves the Hebrew's predicate-first order, which in English reads as archaic or foreign. LOGOS and PNEUMA reorder to standard English. PNEUMA adds an exclamation mark for dramatic emphasis. The Hebrew text has no punctuation marks; the exclamation is interpretive. EMET's rendering of *Elohim* as "Elohim" (transliterated) vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "God" follows their respective conventions.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "This is the finger of God" -- following LOGOS. English subject-predicate order for readability; no exclamation mark (the Hebrew does not signal exclamatory force beyond what the content inherently carries); "God" for *Elohim* per SOPHIA convention.

**Severity:** LOW. The meaning is identical across all three renderings. The disagreement concerns word order, transliteration convention, and punctuation.

---

## 6. v.21 -- *Arov*: "swarm" vs. "swarms of insects" vs. "'arov [swarm]"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'arov [swarm]" | Literal: transliterated with gloss |
| LOGOS | "swarm" | Formal: preserves Hebrew ambiguity |
| PNEUMA | "swarms of insects" | Dynamic: specifies creature type |

**Hebrew:** הֶעָרֹב (*he-'arov*) -- from root *arav* (ערב), "to mix, to mingle." Singular with definite article. Identity debated: mixed insects, flies, wild beasts.

**LXX:** κυνόμυια (*kunomuia*) -- "dog-fly," a specific biting fly.

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA adds "of insects" -- an identification the Hebrew does not make. The root *arav* means "to mix/mingle," and ancient interpretations range from a specific insect (LXX: dog-fly) to mixed wild beasts (Midrash). EMET transliterates the Hebrew term with a gloss, which is maximally accurate but reads as a study note. LOGOS simply renders "swarm," preserving the ambiguity. PNEUMA also uses the plural "swarms" where the Hebrew has the singular *he-'arov*.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "swarm" (singular, unqualified) -- following LOGOS. The Hebrew's ambiguity is preserved. The singular matches the Hebrew singular with definite article. No creature type is specified.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The identification matters for visualization and for understanding the plague's relationship to Egyptian religion. "Swarm of insects" and "mixture of wild beasts" are very different plagues. The Hebrew's ambiguity may be intentional, and the translation should not resolve what the original text leaves open.

---

## 7. v.22 -- *Hifleti*: "set apart" (unanimous agreement)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "set apart" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "set apart" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "set apart" | Dynamic |

**No disagreement.** All three translators render the hiphil of *palah* as "set apart." This is noted for completeness: the Goshen distinction is the chapter's most important theological development, and the translators agree on the rendering. The LXX's *paradoxasō* ("I will make marvelous") adds a wonder-dimension that "set apart" does not fully capture, but all three translators (and SOPHIA) accept the trade-off.

---

## 8. v.23 -- *Pedut*: "redemption" vs. "distinction" vs. "division"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pedut [ransom/division]" | Literal: transliterated with dual gloss |
| LOGOS | "distinction" | Formal: follows LXX *diastolē* and variant *pelut* |
| PNEUMA | "division" | Dynamic: spatial sense, follows variant |

**Hebrew (MT):** פְּדֻת (*pedut*) -- from *padah* (פדה), "to redeem, to ransom."

**Variant:** Some manuscripts and the Samaritan Pentateuch read פְּלֻת (*pelut*) or פְּלוּדָה (*peludah*), "division, separation."

**LXX:** διαστολήν (*diastolēn*) -- "distinction, division" -- appears to follow the variant reading.

**Nature of disagreement:** This is the most significant disagreement in the chapter. The MT reads *pedut* ("redemption/ransom"), but LOGOS and PNEUMA both follow the variant reading *pelut* ("distinction/division") and the LXX. EMET hedges with a dual gloss. The question is whether to follow the MT or the variant/LXX when they diverge.

The MT's *pedut* is theologically richer: the distinction between Israel and Egypt is described as an act of *redemption*. God does not merely separate but *ransoms* his people from under the plagues. This connects the Goshen distinction to the broader exodus theology of redemption (*padah* appears in Deuteronomy 7:8, 9:26, 13:5, etc., always for God's redemptive acts). The variant *pelut* ("division") is simpler and may have arisen because a scribe expected a word meaning "separation" in the context of spatial distinction.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "redemption" -- departing from all three translators. SOPHIA follows the MT, which reads *pedut*. The SOPHIA convention consistently follows the Leningrad Codex as the primary text. While the LXX and variant readings support "distinction/division," the MT's theological reading is not implausible and is in fact more profound: the spatial separation of Goshen from Egypt is itself a redemptive act. The consensus makes the deliberate choice to preserve this MT reading and note the variant.

**Severity:** HIGH. This is a textual-critical decision, not merely a translation choice. Following the MT (*pedut*, "redemption") or the variant (*pelut*, "distinction") produces different theological readings of the Goshen distinction. The SOPHIA convention of following the MT governs the decision, but the issue is genuinely contested.

---

## 9. v.26 -- *To'avah*: "abomination" vs. "detestable"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to'avah [abomination]" | Literal: transliterated with gloss |
| LOGOS | "abomination" | Formal: strong rendering |
| PNEUMA | "detestable" | Dynamic: adjectival, softer |

**Hebrew:** תּוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם (*to'avat Mitsrayim*) -- "the abomination of Egypt." The noun *to'evah* is the strongest term of religious revulsion in Hebrew.

**LXX:** τὰ βδελύγματα τῶν Αἰγυπτίων (*ta bdelugmata tōn Aiguptiōn*) -- "the abominations of the Egyptians." *Bdelugma* is the standard LXX rendering of *to'evah* and carries the same force.

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA renders the noun *to'evah* as the adjective "detestable," changing the part of speech and softening the force. "Detestable" describes a quality; "abomination" names a category. Moses' argument depends on the categorical nature of the offense: what Israel sacrifices *is* the abomination of Egypt -- a thing that belongs to the class of the abominable. "Detestable to the Egyptians" (PNEUMA's full phrase) makes it a matter of subjective feeling; "the abomination of Egypt" makes it a matter of objective religious category.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "abomination" -- following LOGOS. The noun form preserves the Hebrew's categorical force. The LXX confirms the strong rendering.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The essential meaning is conveyed by both renderings (the Egyptians would be outraged), but the rhetorical force differs. Moses is not saying "they won't like it"; he is saying "it is categorically abominable to them."

---

## 10. v.28 -- Infinitive absolute: "going far you shall not go far" vs. "do not go far"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going far you shall not go far" | Literal: preserves infinitive absolute construction |
| LOGOS | "do not go far -- going far" | Formal: reversed order, dash |
| PNEUMA | "only do not go far!" | Dynamic: simplifies, drops infinitive absolute |

**Hebrew:** רַק הַרְחֵק לֹא־תַרְחִיקוּ לָלֶכֶת (*raq harchek lo-tarchiku lalechet*) -- infinitive absolute *harchek* + negative + finite verb *tarchiku* + infinitive *lalechet* ("to go").

**Nature of disagreement:** The infinitive absolute is a Hebrew emphatic construction with no direct English equivalent. EMET preserves the Hebrew word order (infinitive absolute first); LOGOS reverses it (finite verb first, infinitive absolute as afterthought); PNEUMA eliminates it entirely, producing a simple prohibition. The question is how much awkwardness in English is justified by fidelity to the Hebrew rhetorical device.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "going far you shall not go far" -- following EMET. The Hebrew's emphasis is on the *distance* -- Pharaoh repeats the root *r-ch-q* (far) to underscore his anxiety. PNEUMA's simplification loses this emphasis entirely. LOGOS's reversal weakens it. The slight awkwardness in English signals the emphatic Hebrew construction.

**Severity:** LOW. The meaning (do not go far) is clear in all three renderings. The disagreement concerns how much rhetorical emphasis to preserve.

---

## 11. v.6 -- Collective singular "the frog" vs. plural "the frogs"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the frog came up" | Literal: preserves collective singular |
| LOGOS | "the frogs came up" | Formal: normalizes to English plural |
| PNEUMA | "the frogs came up" | Dynamic: normalizes to English plural |

**Hebrew:** וַתַּעַל הַצְּפַרְדֵּעַ (*vata'al ha-tsefardea'*) -- "and the frog came up." Singular noun with singular verb.

**LXX:** ἀνεβιβάσθη ὁ βάτραχος (*anebibisthē ho batrachos*) -- "the frog was brought up." Also singular.

**Nature of disagreement:** The Hebrew uses a collective singular -- one "frog" representing the entire plague as a single mass. This is a grammatically marked choice; the plural *tsefarde'im* is available and used elsewhere in the chapter. LOGOS and PNEUMA normalize to the English plural, which is the natural rendering but erases the Hebrew's deliberate grammatical choice. EMET preserves the singular.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the frog came up" -- following EMET. The collective singular is a deliberate literary device in the Hebrew, confirmed by the LXX's preservation of the singular. The shift to plural in subsequent verses heightens the effect: the one becomes many. English readers can grasp the collective singular (compare "the enemy approached" or "the locust came").

**Severity:** LOW. The plague's meaning is unchanged. The disagreement concerns whether to preserve an unusual Hebrew grammatical feature.

---

## 12. Throughout -- Recurring convention disagreements

The following disagreements recur from previous chapters and are noted briefly:

**a. Divine name:** EMET and PNEUMA use YHWH; LOGOS uses "the LORD." SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "The River" vs. "Nile" vs. "Ye'or":** EMET: "Ye'or"; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Nile." SOPHIA follows "the River" per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

---

*Twelve disagreements logged for Exodus 8. Two rated HIGH (hitpa'er rendering in v. 9; pedut text-critical decision in v. 23), three MODERATE (shalach phrasing in v. 1; arov identification in v. 21; to'avah rendering in v. 26), seven LOW (kaved word order in v. 15; kinnim identification in vv. 16-17; finger of God word order in v. 19; infinitive absolute in v. 28; collective singular in v. 6; three recurring conventions). The dominant pattern continues: EMET and LOGOS converge on literal/formal renderings; PNEUMA diverges toward interpretive paraphrase. In this chapter, SOPHIA notably departs from all three translators on the pedut question (v. 23), rendering "redemption" where EMET hedges, LOGOS renders "distinction," and PNEUMA renders "division." SOPHIA also follows PNEUMA against EMET/LOGOS on the hitpa'er question (v. 9), rendering "The honor is yours" for the sake of intelligibility -- a rare dynamic-over-literal decision.*

--- SOPHIA
